g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on the DDA Algorithm, a fundamental line drawing technique in computer graphics.
Raster displays use pixels in a grid. We need algorithms to determine which pixels best approximate an ideal mathematical line.
g.text: Why Do We Need Line Drawing Algorithms?
In raster displays, lines must be

g.data-narration: The DDA algorithm uses incremental calculations, adding constant values instead of performing expensive multiplications at each step.

svg.g:
What is DDA Algorithm? The Digital Differential Analyzer (DDA) is an incremental line drawing algorithm that computes pixel positions by adding constant increments, rather than calculating y = mx + b at each step (which requires multiplication).
Traditional (Multiplication) y = mx + b (expensive!)
<g transform="translate="560, 220">
DDA (Addition) y = y + yinc (fast!)

g.data-narration: The DDA calculates the slope between endpoints, then uses the number of steps to determine increment values for x and y coordinates.
For gentle slopes (|m| ≤ 1), we step along x-axis. For steep slopes (|m| > 1), we step along y-axis to avoid gaps in the line.
g.text: Decision Rule: Which Axis to Step?

g.data-narration: The complete DDA procedure involves reading endpoints, calculating differences and increments, then iteratively plotting pixels by adding constants.
The key insight is incremental calculation: instead of multiplying each time, we simply add the constant increment to the previous value.

g.text: y = mx + b at EVERY step

g.data-narration: Let's draw a line from (2,3) to (8,6). Since |Δx|=6 > |Δy|=3, we step along x with increments xinc=1, yinc=0.5.
For a steep line from (1,2) to (3,9), since |Δy|=7 > |Δx|=2, we step along y with yinc=1 and xinc≈0.286.

g.text: This prevents gaps in steep lines
If we stepped in X: large gaps between pixels

g.data-narration: For horizontal line from (2,4) to (7,4), Δy=0, so steps=|Δx|=5, yinc=0. We simply step through x values incrementing by 1.
DDA uses floating-point arithmetic which is slower than integer operations and can accumulate rounding errors over long distances.

g.text: Uses float arithmetic, rounding for pixels
DDA is conceptually simpler → Great for learning! Forms foundation for Bresenham's algorithm.

g.data-narration: The DDA handles all line orientations: positive/negative slopes, horizontal, vertical, and diagonal lines by appropriately selecting the stepping axis.
g.text: DDA handles all these by comparing |Δx| and |Δy| and
stepping along the axis with the larger change.

g.data-narration: Remember these essential formulas: slope m = Δy/Δx, steps = max(|Δx|,|Δy|), increments = Δ/steps, and always round coordinates when plotting.
In exams: always check slope magnitude first, use max(|Δx|,|Δy|) for steps, remember to round coordinates, and understand why Bresenham is preferred in practice.

g.text: ✓ Always check |m| ≤ 1 or |m| > 1 FIRST to determine stepping axis
✓ Always round() coordinates before plotting pixels
✓ Key advantage: Incremental calculation replaces multiplication with addition
✓ DDA is conceptually simpler — great for learning
✓ Bresenham is preferred in practice (integer-only, no errors)
Master DDA first → then learn Bresenham's
Practice with different line examples to master DDA
